title: Internships
full_text:
Internships
An internship is short-term, supervised work experience with a professional organization to get real-world experience in your chosen field or career. Each one includes intentional learning objectives, on-the-job training, and guidance and mentorship from internship hosts.
Internships are required for many Associate of Applied Science (AAS) degrees. Some are offered through FVCC and others are available directly through employers.
Programs with Required Internships
: Business Administration, Graphic Design, Information Technology, Web Technology
Programs with Optional Internships
: Accounting Technology, Criminal Justice, Small Business Management, Health Care Office Management, Programming and Game Development, Computer Science
Independent Study Internship
: You may have the option to participate in an independent study internship. Discuss options with your faculty advisor and Career Services.
Micro-Internships
: Like traditional internships, micro-internships take place any semester and are short-term, professional assignments. Typically, micro-internships are performed remotely 5-40 hours of work total over a week or month time period.
Search and apply for a micro-internship.
Internship FAQ
Plan to meet with an advisor for an internship orientation the semester before you hope to intern.
Generally, this should be by November 1 for a Spring semester internship, July 1 for a Fall semester internship, and April 1 for a Summer semester internship.
Internships can be both paid and unpaid. If you find or currently have a job within your field, check with your advisor if it is possible to count this as your internship.
Discuss potential internship sites with your advisor and career advisor. All internship sites undergo an approval process to ensure that you will receive appropriate training and apply your knowledge and skills.
Students may not intern at family’s business nor with an organization where a student’s family member or significant other ranks above the student’s supervisor.
As an FVCC college course, start and end dates for the internship follow the same semester begin and end dates as other classes. Your goal should be to have a signed Internship Agreement in place with an agreed upon start date before the semester begins.
Hours at the worksite are determined according to academic guidelines based on the number of course credits.
The credit ratio is one credit for approximately every 45 hours. Interns spend 135-150 hours at their worksites for a 3-credit course, usually working about 10-15 hours per week, although this may vary.
Assignments vary depending upon the instructor.
Most faculty monitor your learning through a weekly activity log, or learning objectives assignment. Many will conduct a mid-term site visit and ask that your internship provider submit a mid-term and final evaluation.
You will receive a grade. Read the Student Internship Handbook and Internship Agreement for more information.
You register and pay tuition for internship credits just like all other credit courses at FVCC.
Student Resources
Internship Agreement & Monthly Timesheet
Student starts the agreement after instructor approval of internship and submits timesheet at the end of each month.
All emails
must be accurate
for the forms to advance to the internship employer and/or instructor.

noreply@fvcc.edu
.

title: Apprenticeships
full_text:
Apprenticeships
Start your career by taking classes at FVCC while gaining on-the-job training.
Apprenticeships are proven hands-on training programs designed to provide industry and business-specific skills necessary for a successful career.
An apprenticeship combines on-the-job training with technical education plus scheduled wage increases: the more skills and knowledge you gain, the more money you make.
Current Apprenticeship Programs
Registered Electrical Apprentice C.T.S.
Carpenter Apprenticeship Program (a non-credit program sponsored by Swank Enterprises)
Registered Plumbing Apprenticeship Program
Complete the FVCC Apprenticeship Interest form to get started.

FAQ
Apprenticeship works when an apprentice (you) is employed by a sponsoring business that provides paid on-the-job training that coincides with classroom learning.
The apprentice and sponsoring business agree on the terms and conditions of the Apprenticeship Standards, which the employer uses to register their apprenticeship program with the Montana Registered Apprenticeship Program.
The Apprenticeship Standards lays out the wage scale, length of apprenticeship, work process schedule and competencies, related instruction, and responsibilities of both the employer and the apprentice.
Begin the career you want while taking FVCC classes and learn essential skills while earning a salary.
With a progressive wage scale, the more you learn in the classroom and on the job, the more money you make. Many employers pay for the educational costs of their apprentices or offer tuition reimbursement.
According to Montana Registered Apprenticeship, apprentices earn an average of $20,000 more than the state-wide average wage - a difference of $600,000 over a 30-year career.
Anyone looking to begin a new career or upgrade their skills to advance in their current job can apprentice.

title: Dean of Students
full_text:
Dean of Students
Every student deserves a positive educational experience. The Dean of Students is here to ensure that your experience at FVCC is positive and to address any issues that may arise.
The Dean of Students is a primary resource for connecting students who are navigating personal, academic or other situations to supportive campus and community resources. The Dean of Students will discuss the situation with you and provide guidance on available options.
All reports made to the Dean of Students will be taken seriously and information will be provided to the proper office for review.
As members of the campus community, it is your choice whether to report an incident. The Dean of Students will support you during this process by providing information, support and advocacy.
Copies of the Code of Student Conduct, Student Conduct Procedures, Student Complaint Procedures, and Student Complaint Form may be obtained from the Dean of Students or online in the
Board Policies
section.
Policies and Procedures
Students at FVCC are expected to uphold their academic responsibilities. Academic misconduct is also known as academic dishonesty and academic integrity.
If you are a student, faculty, or staff member, please view the procedures below to file a report regarding an academic integrity violation. If you have any questions, please contact the Dean of Students.
Academic Integrity Procedures (PDF)
The FVCC CARE Team
reviews reports about student behavior and determines the best course of action to support the student and intervene before the behavior escalates.
The mission is to promote a safe and productive learning, living and working environment by addressing the needs of students through coordination and assessment of information and developing a supportive plan.
If you sense something that does not seem right, the team asks that you submit a CARE Referral or contact the FVCC CARE coordinator at (406) 756-3812.
A student has the right to lodge a complaint or grievance. FVCC is committed to ensuring that all concerns and complaints of students are addressed fairly and are resolved promptly.
To file a student complaint, please view the procedures below. Reach out to the Dean of Students for assistance or if you have questions.
Student Complaint Procedure
Flathead Valley Community College students are responsible for knowing the information, policies, and procedures outlined in college policies.
The Code of Student Conduct and the
Student Conduct Procedure
apply to the conduct of individual students and all college affiliated student organizations.
The Dean of Students coordinates and facilitates the conduct process in response to an incident that violated the Code of Student Conduct.
The Code of Student Conduct and Student Complaints is located in
FVCC Board Policy Chapter VII Section 60
(PDF).
For more information about Title IX and the FVCC Discrimination, Harassment, Sexual Misconduct, Stalking and Retaliation policy and procedure visit the page below.
Title IX and Sexual Misconduct
Reporting Forms
Student Complaint
Students use this to initiate a formal complaint per the Student Complaint Procedures (view below).
CARE Referral
This form is to be used by anyone to share information about a student of concern. The CARE Team provides support.
Academic Misconduct Report
Faculty and others use this form to report a Student Academic Misconduct (integrity) concern.
Report an Incident
This form is to be used by students, staff, and faculty to report alleged violations of the Code of Student Conduct.
Title IX and Sexual Misconduct
Faculty, staff and students use this form to report incidents involving discrimination and/or sexual misconduct.
Resources
FVCC Care Team
The FVCC CARE Team reviews reports about student behavior and determines the best course of action to support the student and intervene before the behavior escalates.
Title IX and Sexual Misconduct
Information about Title IX and the FVCC Discrimination, Harassment, Sexual Misconduct, Stalking and Retaliation policy and procedure.
Contact the Dean of Students for support and consultation via phone, email and appointments between 8:30am-4pm Monday-Friday.
The Dean of Students may communicate with students through a communication system called Maxient.  If you receive a notification that you have a Maxient letter for pickup, please ensure you read that communication promptly.
Reach the Dean of Students at
deanofstudents@fvcc.edu
or (406) 756-3812

Off-campus Access
Accessing materials off-campus looks different.
Find directions here.
Library Spaces & Services
Study rooms, classrooms and the Makerspace can only be reserved by FVCC students, faculty and staff for campus-related activities.
Reserve a Library Room
Materials can be checked out using a student or staff ID or an FVCC issued library card. Books check out for 28 days and DVDs check out for 14 days.
If you are an outside library looking to borrow from FVCC please contact Chris Vernon at
cvernon@fvcc.edu
or
(406) 756-3870
Sometimes you need copies of books, articles, media, or other items that are not located in our library or databases. Submit a request and library staff will work with Montana Academic Libraries (via TRAILS Resource Sharing) or other libraries across the country (via Interlibrary Loan) to get the items you need from another library. Books and DVDs are also available from other TRAILS Resource Sharing and ILL. These materials have special due dates assigned by the lending library.
Submit a TRAILS Resource Sharing request through your FVCC Library account.
Request an Interlibrary Loan
(ILL). You will be notified via phone or email when the item arrives.
Course
Reserves
Faculty often place course reading materials and supplemental resources on reserve at the Circulation Desk. Some materials are for in-library use only.
Textbook
Reserves
The library has a small collection of current textbooks available for use in the library. These cannot be checked out, to allow consistent access for everyone.
Technology
Desktop computers are available for use. Specialized software has been loaded onto some machines. If you have a particular software need, please inquire at the Circulation Desk. Wi-Fi is available for all patrons. The library offers computers and a limited number of hotspots for student check out.
Assistive
Technology Station
The library has a station with specialized hardware and technology to help users with visual or mobility needs.
Printing/Copying
A scanner/color copier, laser printer, and some general office supplies are available for patron use. Personal computers and mobile devices can connect to the wireless network and can be used for PDF wireless printing.
Printing
in
Black and White
Students receive up to 500 pages per semester. Contact the Business Office to purchase more.
Community members pay $0.10 per side at the circulation desk.
Printing
in Color
Students and community members must pay for color copies. Color copies are $0.35 per side (8.5×11). The machine accepts cash and coins. Copy cards can also be purchased at Common Ground or the Business Office.
The FVCC Library is the central repository for all lost and found items on campus. If you find something, please bring it to the Circulation Desk. If you lost something, fill out our
Lost & Found form
or call us at 406.756.3856.
The FVCC Writing Lab (BL 213), located in the Broussard Family Library & Learning Commons, is available to all students for free assistance in writing.
Schedule online with us.
We provide students help in:
One-on-one tutoring in English skills, e.g., grammar/punctuation
One-on-one tutoring on research papers and essays, e.g., introductions, organization, conclusions
Assistance with MLA, APA, Chicago, and other writing formats (handouts available)
Word processing assistance
PowerPoint assistance
Computers and printers
Internet searches
Reference books, e.g., dictionaries, thesauri, English handbooks
Revising, editing, and proofreading of essays in
all
disciplines
Access student portal & Eagle Mail
Connection to library databases, e.g., Montana Shared Catalog, Gale, SIRS Researcher
Assistance with résumés and cover letters
Scholarship letters
Transfer letters/essays
Set defaults on laptops

title: About the Library
full_text:
About the Library
Flathead Valley Community College Library promotes and provides access to quality information resources and services to promote excellence in lifelong learning, student success, and support community needs.
Vision Statement 1: Increase Access to Education
Vision Statement 2: Increase Student Academic Success and Information Literacy
Vision Statement 3: Provide Diverse Educational Opportunities
Location
The FVCC Library is open to anyone in the community. Visit the Broussard Family Library & Learning Commons adjacent to the Broussard Center for Nursing & Health Science at the south edge of campus.
TRAILS Consortium
FVCC is part of a statewide network with fellow Montana Academic Libraries to create the Treasure State Academic Information & Library Services (TRAILS) group. This means patrons have access to a broad expanse of materials from across the state through
Resource Sharing
.
Policies and Circulation
Obtaining a Library Card
Loan Period
Fines
Students, Faculty, & Staff
Use your FVCC student or staff ID card as your library card. If you do not have a student or staff ID you can obtain a library card at the Circulation desk. This card must be present each time you check out materials.
Community Users
Obtain a free library card by providing the following:
valid Montana ID showing proof of Flathead or Lincoln County residency and current mailing address (utility bill can be substituted if necessary) and
secondary contact person’s mailing address and phone number.
Please note the secondary contact cannot live at the same address or share the same number as the applicant.
Books check out for 28 days; DVD’s and video cassettes check out for 14 days. Materials requested from other libraries have special due dates assigned by the lending library which are noted on the item. Extensions may be requested via phone, email, in-person, or online chat, as available.
The FVCC Library will assess fines for overdue library materials in an effort to achieve the timely return of loaned items to the collection. The fines will be assessed as follows: books and DVDs are $0.10 per day accumulating to a maximum of $10.00 per item.
The borrower is responsible for any lost or damaged item(s). There will be a replacement cost of the item(s) plus a processing fee of $5.00 charged to the account.
For students, the library will notify the business office of the fine and flag the student’s record. Grades and transcripts will be held until the fines are paid and the account restored.
For community users, outstanding balances not settled directly with the library will be submitted to a local collection agency for recovery, along with service fees from the agency.

title: TRIO Support
full_text:
TRIO Support
FVCC TRIO provides comprehensive academic support to selected eligible students. Your personal and academic success is our goal.
Students are automatically screened for TRIO once they apply and are admitted to FVCC. Funded by the U.S. Department of Education, TRIO offers a wide range of services and a place for students to call home.
Your TRIO resources:
Personalized academic advising and transfer planning
Career and major exploration
Visits to UM and MSU
Financial aid application (FAFSA) and scholarship assistance
FREE college guidance classes (COLS 102: College Success Strategies and COLS 110: College to Career)
Dedicated TRIO student space, the First Gen Den, staffed by student mentors
TRIO community events and activities
Montana American Indian Scholars program and scholarships
To qualify for TRIO, you must:
Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident
Be an FVCC degree-seeking student
Have an academic need for TRIO services
In addition, you must meet one or more of the following requirements:
Be eligible through income guidelines as defined by the U.S. Department of Education
Be from a family in which neither parent/guardian has earned a bachelor's degree
Be eligible to receive accommodations through FVCC Office of Disability Services
Questions?
Stop by the TRIO offices in the Student Center (SC) Room 102 or contact us at
trio@fvcc.edu
or (406) 756-3880 or (406) 756-4358.
Apply for TRIO
Also In This Section

title: TRIO Tutoring Program
full_text:
TRIO Tutoring Program
TRIO Tutoring Program is a FREE academic service for eligible students. Learn whether you
qualify for TRIO
.
Connect with a Virtual Tutor
TRIO tutoring is provided through TutorMe; 24/7 tutoring in over 300 subjects. TutorMe tutors are professionally trained and sessions are conducted in real-time with a live tutor using a video call format. Other tutoring arrangements, including group tutoring, can also be possible.
How to Connect with a TutorMe Tutor
Join a Weekly TRIO Study Jam
Get help at free weekly review sessions for historically difficult courses. Meet with people in your class to compare notes, discuss important concepts, develop strategies for studying, and to test yourselves in preparation for in-class exams. Contact TRIO tutoring coordinator for current study jam schedules or creating a study jam at
trio@fvcc.edu
or stop by SC 102.
Talk with a TRIO Student Mentor
Visit the First Gen Den in LRC 102 to talk with a TRIO mentor about study skills and success strategies. Mentors may be able to assist with additional tutoring.
Questions?
Stop by the TRIO offices in the Student Center (SC) Room 102 or contact us at
trio@fvcc.edu
or (406) 756-3880 or (406) 756-4358.
Apply for TRIO

title: American Indian Student Resources
full_text:
American Indian Student Resources
Montana American Indian Scholars
Inspired by a statewide initiative through the Montana Office of the Commissioner of Higher Education, and under the TRIO umbrella, is the Montana American Indian Scholars Program (MAIS). Program goals include connecting American Indian students to campus resources, creating a welcoming environment for students and their families, building community and promoting cultural awareness.
Scholarships
MAIS offers four $500 scholarships each year to American Indian students who are interested in the opportunity to build mentoring and leadership skills while working closely with MAIS and TRIO staff. Leadership opportunities may include: attendance at TRIO events, mentoring students, participation in campus tours, membership in the Native American Student Association student club, and/or event planning.
Connect with Leslie Greene for more information at
lgreene@fvcc.edu
or visit TRIO at LRC 102.

title: Testing Center
full_text:
Testing Center
The testing center provides FVCC students and the local and surrounding communities with a secure, distraction-free environment to take exams. The test center is located in the Student Center (SC) Room 123A.
Test proctoring is FREE for current students. Other exams, including professional exams and exams for other colleges and universities, require testing fees.
Scheduling Instructions
Test proctoring for computer-based or written tests is available to all FVCC students, including Eagle Online and campus-based courses. Testing for other colleges, universities and various other professional or certification tests can be accommodated if this location is deemed valid by the testing institution.
Schedule an On-Campus Exam
Scheduling an on-campus exam
must occur at least 48 hours in advance of your test.
For booking at
Lincoln County Campus (LCC)
select “Libby Campus” from the drop-down menu and indicate if the test is written or if a computer will be needed in the Special Needs field.
Book Off-site Testing
If you are located outside of Flathead County, you may choose an off-site proctor for exams that require proctoring. You are responsible for locating a suitable proctor and submitting the candidate’s information for approval.
Complete and submit a
Proctor Request form
.
Adequate time is needed to process and validate proctors, so please apply within the first two weeks of the semester.
Please submit all requests for proctoring (online or hybrid courses only)
before the semester begins or immediately after
. This enables scheduling of resources and spaces, as well as convenience for the students to schedule their test times as soon as they want.
Please complete a
faculty request for proctoring
for each test.
Proctoring is available for some workforce/professional certification tests. FVCC is an authorized testing center for PearsonVUE, Measurelearning and some specific Prometric tests (ASE and The Institutes). If you are interested in taking your certification exam at the FVCC Test Center, follow these steps:
Register for the exam using the testing agency’s website. FVCC does NOT register candidates for PearsonVUE or Measurelearning professional exams.
Choose Flathead Valley Community College as the test location. FVCC will appear as an option if authorized to deliver the test. Testing security requirements may limit the locations available for particular tests.
On the day of the exam, bring your registration confirmation and the items necessary for admittance as listed, including a valid picture ID with a signature.
In the state of Montana, the high school diploma equivalency can be achieved through HiSET or GED
.
When you are ready to start testing, visit
hiset.org
or
ged.com
to create an account, register and pay for testing. Your name and date of birth on your account must match your government-issued photo ID.
To find testing locations and available dates, check your account. All tests are computer-based. In-person testing for the HiSET and GED is available at FVCC, while the Libby campus offers only the HiSET.
On testing day, make sure to bring your valid government-issued photo ID and $5 per test receipt from the Business Office to the testing center.
For any other questions regarding test sign up, please contact us at
adulted@fvcc.edu
.
Procedures for Proctored Tests
Please view the following procedures prior to using testing facilities. By making an appointment for proctoring services, you are verifying that you have read and understand the procedures.
Please arrive 10 minutes prior to the scheduled appointment time.
Students must provide a valid photo identification card – FVCC or testing institution student ID, state issued ID or driver’s license, passport, or military ID.
No personal items are allowed in the testing area and all personal items brought to the testing area will be stored in a secure location. These items include but are not limited to cell phones, wallets, purses, backpacks, outer wear, books/notes (unless allowed by instructor), laptops, tablets and other personal electronic devices, and outside/personal scratch paper.
No food or drinks are allowed in the testing area.
No persons, including children, other than the student and no pets are allowed in the testing area. Service animals are permitted.
Academic integrity is integral to the mission of the testing center; academic misconduct will not be tolerated and instructors will be notified of any issues.
If you need to cancel or reschedule your appointment,
you may do so by using the link provided in your original confirmation email. If you have any questions or difficulties, please contact
proctor@fvcc.edu
or
(406) 756-3888
.
*Policies and Procedures for off-site proctored tests may vary.
If you have any questions or need assistance, connect with the Test Center at proctor@fvcc.edu or 406.756.3880.
Email Us

title: Information Technology (IT)
full_text:
Information Technology (IT)
The Information Technology Department is dedicated to delivering high quality technical service to the students, employees, and community. It is our goal to continuously meet the technological needs of all students, staff, faculty, and visitors.
IT Support Services
We offer direct support to employees and students through the IT Support Office. Support hours are Monday - Friday 8-5 on days the college is open. The IT Supoort office is located in Blake Hall 160.
We provide new employees or students a login ID and password to access computers, email, and web portal.
We assist in resolving hardware, software, and connectivity issues in all campus buildings.
We support and maintain software and computers throughout the college, including computer labs.
We preserve data integrity in our network environments.
We work with faculty in support of technology enhanced classes.
Eagle Online and Eagle Mail support
Contact Us

.

In emergency situations call 9-1-1 or go to the nearest Emergency Room.
In-Person Mental Health Counseling
Counseling at FVCC is free to students enrolled in credit courses. Counseling is confidential and is not part of a student’s academic record.
To schedule an appointment or for additional information, please email

Counseling offers support for whatever you may be going through like emotional and personal concerns, stress and anxiety, adjusting to school and life changes, and overcoming academic barriers.

title: Student Wellness Team
full_text:
Student Wellness Team
We are a team of students serving as health and wellness resources for our fellow FVCC students and community.
We strive to foster a campus atmosphere that supports and respects healthy lifestyle choices in areas such as fitness, nutrition, emotional health, sexual health, tobacco prevention, alcohol and drugs use, and chronic illnesses like diabetes and asthma.
Team Goals
As a student run and led team, we aim to:
Create a better understanding of health and wellness on campus
Help all students make healthy lifestyle choices on campus
Make healthy lifestyle choices more appealing and socially accepted
Develop systems and structures to support student health and wellness
Check out weekly wellness tips from Campus Well
Join the Team
Are you motivated to share and develop wellness resources on campus? We welcome any current students interested in joining the Student Wellness Team to
apply here
.

title: Student Health Clinic
full_text:
Student Health Clinic
If you are experiencing a life threatening emergency (chest pain, difficulty breathing, severe bleeding) call 9-1-1.
The FVCC Student Health Clinic is here to help students on campus address health and wellness concerns. Call the Student Health Clinic at 406.756.4331 for an appointment.
Hours and Location
Monday, Wednesday & Thursday: 9am-2:30pm
(Closed on holidays and breaks)
Summer Hours:
Monday & Wednesday, 9am-2:30pm
Appointments and walk-ins are welcome.
Located next to the library in the Broussard Center, Room 136.
View Campus Map
Services
Services include urgent care and primary health care like health evaluations, immunizations, TB testing, and DOT physicals; treatment of minor injuries and acute health problems such as colds, flu, bladder infections, sprains, and strains; and reproductive health evaluations, including PAPs, contraception, and STI testing.
Health evaluations, treatment of minor injuries and acute health problems such as colds, flu, bladder infections, sprains, and strains

Medical, surgical and dental referrals
Limited in-office laboratory testing provided free of charge (mono, strep, flu, urinary infections, pregnancy and COVID testing). Other laboratory tests and all x-rays are referred into the community and payment is the student’s responsibility.

Shelley Naomi MS, PA-C
Clinic Provider-Physician Assistant
Shelley Naomi, PA is a native of Greeley, CO. She graduated from the University of Northern Colorado in 1997 and received her Master of Science in Physician Assistant Studies from Arizona School of Health Science, Mesa AZ in 2000. She has many years of experience as a physician assistant in family practice, urgent care and emergency medicine. Shelley is an avid reader, enjoys volleyball, volunteering in her church and community and spending time with her family.
Kathy Weller
Clinic Medical Assistant
Kathy Weller was born and raised in Arvada, CO and lived in Billings, MT for 10 years prior to moving to Lakeside in 2005. Kathy is a medical assistant with experience in various practices including urology, orthopedics, internal medicine, family practice and veterinary. She is married and has 3 grown children. Kathy loves animals, and enjoys cross country skiing, snowshoeing, camping, crafting, boating and hiking.
Mona Cuthbert, MD
Medical Director-Physician
Mona Cuthbert, MD is a family practice physician who currently oversees the clinic as medical director. Originally from Alaska, she attended University of Washington School of Medicine and returned to Alaska to work with the Alaska Native population for 10 years. She was also the medical director at UAA Student Health in Anchorage. Currently she lives in Kalispell with her husband and three children and enjoys working doing family practice and women’s health care. Outside of work she loves all things outdoors, including hiking, skiing, backpacking and biking.

title: Food Pantry
full_text:
Food Pantry
The FVCC Food Pantry is designed to meet the immediate needs of students by providing non-perishable food items and personal hygiene products. By providing students and their families with basic needs, students can continue to succeed in the classroom and beyond.
To Utilize the FVCC Food Pantry
The FVCC Food Pantry provides a free service to students currently enrolled in classes, who need emergency food assistance. Please stop by the pantry located in SC 109 during open hours or connect with us at
food-pantry@fvcc.edu
or 406.756.3908 or stop by the Student Engagement Office in the Student Center, Room 108. Student identification is required. Privacy is protected and all information is kept confidential.
Get Involved with the Food Pantry
Thank you for your interest in the FVCC Food Pantry. There are multiple ways you can help.
Donate Items
We accept non-perishable food, hygiene items and grocery store gift cards. Blue donation boxes are located by the Student Engagement Office in the Student Center, Room 109. We would greatly appreciate any of the items listed: canned fruits and vegetables, canned or instant potatoes, fruit and vegetable juices, vegetable broth, dried fruit, instant rice, applesauce cups, canned meat (chicken, tuna, etc.), nuts (peanuts, almonds, cashews, etc.), peanut or other nut butters, dried cereal, dried oats or instant oatmeal, dried pasta (spaghetti, macaroni, etc.), spices or other sauces, jelly, plastic bags & can openers.
Donate Funds
If you would like to make a monetary donation, please contact the FVCC Foundation Office at 406.756.3963.
Student Volunteers
Student
volunteers
may organize and stock the pantry, promote the food pantry, and assist clients. For information about volunteering, please contact the Student Engagement Office at student-engagement@fvcc.edu.
Work-Study
There are
work-study opportunities
with the Food Pantry. If you are eligible for work-study funding, please consider applying for a job in the food pantry
here
.

title: Disability Support Services
full_text:
Disability Support Services
The Disability Support Services office is committed to ensuring equal access to FVCC classes, programs, facilities, and activities for our students with disabilities. The college prohibits discrimination against qualified persons with disabilities.
DSS can help:
Coordinate reasonable accommodations for qualified students
Advocate for an accessible and inclusive learning environment
Promote self-advocacy
These laws provide civil rights protection at FVCC for people with disabilities:
Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act
The Americans with Disabilities Act
The Montana Human Rights Act
Student Resources
FVCC Disability Services Manual
Accommodation Request For
m
Disability Verification For
m
Students with Disabilities Preparing for Postsecondary Education
Educational Apps-Resources to Support Learning
Request for Testing with Approved DSS Accommodation
s
Qualifying for Services
To qualify for Disability Support Services at FVCC, students must request accommodations and provide current documentation of a disability from a licensed physician, psychologist, or other qualified professional. Some examples of documentation include, but are not limited to, medical assessments, psychological evaluations, correspondence from counselors or physicians, and high school Individual Education Plans (IEPs) or 504 Plans that reflect your diagnosis, potential barriers, and functional limitations.
If you believe that you may have an undiagnosed disability, you are encouraged to meet with the DSS coordinator. While FVCC does not provide or pay for medical or psychological testing, Disability Support Services may be able to connect you to community resources that can establish whether a disability is present.
Once a student requests accommodations and provides verification of their disability, the DSS office will schedule an intake meeting with the student. Using the documents provided by the student and the information gathered during the intake meeting, DSS will determine reasonable accommodations, supports and services that can help a student to access FVCC classes, programs, facilities, and activities.
Accommodations
Depending on a student’s disability and the functional limitations attributed to the disability, accommodations could include supports and services such as audiobooks, voice-typing, accessible seating, registration support, extended test time, low-vision magnifier, or ASL interpreting.
For more detailed information regarding Disability Support Services see the FVCC
Disability Support Services Manua
l
, or connect with the coordinator at
disabilitysupport@fvcc.edu
or (406) 756-3376.

title: Veterans Center
full_text:
Veterans Center
FVCC Veterans Center is here to help you meet your personal and academic goals as an FVCC student.
Returning to civilian life after active duty is an adjustment. FVCC has dedicated staff members and faculty attuned to the unique challenges when transitioning between the military and civilian environment. Some staff members are veterans themselves and are available to help you adjust to college life.
FVCC is a designated 2026-2027 Military Friendly® School
Veterans Benefits
Veterans, service members, dependents and spouses may be eligible for more than one “chapter” of education benefits. Each chapter has different eligibility requirements and benefits.
Apply for the GI Bill
Yellow Ribbon Program
FVCC participates in the Yellow Ribbon Program for those veterans using Ch.33, eligible at the 100% rate and are being charged out-of-state tuition. The college accepts 50 students on a first-come, first-served basis. Visit gibill.va.gov for the most updated information on the program.
FVCC Yellow Ribbon Application
Veterans Upward Bound
Veterans Upward Bound is a U.S. Department of Education program designed to help military veterans refresh their academic skills so they can successfully complete post-secondary education. The courses offered at FVCC include Introduction to Computers, Introduction to College Math and Algebra and Introduction to College Writing and Grammar Review.
For more information, connect with the
FVCC Continuing Education Center
.
FVCC Veterans Association
The FVCC Veterans Association is a service support-oriented student organization with the primary objective of developing a foundation of understanding between veterans and non-veterans.
Organizational activities have included active support of the Montana Veterans Home, bi-weekly meetings, fundraising, direct support of other student organization activities and internal social events.
All students, veterans and non-veterans are encouraged to participate as members of the association.
Military Residency
FVCC limits academic residency to 25 percent or less of the degree requirements for all degrees for active-duty service members and their adult family members (spouses and college-age children). In addition, there are no “final year” or “final semester” residency requirements for active-duty service members and their family members. Reservist and National Guardsmen on active-duty are covered in the same manner.
As of August 1, 2021, the Isakson and Roe Veterans Health Care and Benefits Improvement Act of 2020 allows veterans and dependents who are using veterans’ education benefits to have in-state tuition, eliminating the previous 3-year time limit of the Choice Act.

title: Adult Education
full_text:
Adult Education
Build your skills to enter college, training programs or the workforce through free programs offered to residents in Flathead, Lincoln, Lake and Sanders counties. Both in-person and online services are available to anyone ages 16 and up. We're here to help you achieve your education and career goals.
Contact us at
AdultEd@fvcc.edu
or 406.756.3884 to get started.
English Language Learning
English Language Learning (ELL) classes teach adult, non-native speakers of English to communicate through speaking, reading and writing.
High School Equivalency
High School Equivalency Test prep classes are available to community members ages 16 and up.
FAQs
Anyone 16 and up can participate in our programs.
Individuals 16-18 years old must submit
high school or homeschool withdrawal letters, as well as other forms depending on age. Contact us for more details.
All of FVCC Adult Education services are free to Montana residents 16+. We require a pre-assessment for our programs to determine service needs. FVCC offers a scholarship for students wanting to obtain their high school equivalency tests.
Contact us to get scheduled for an intake and pre-assessment. Students 16 years old must meet with the director and a parent/guardian to go over required documentation prior to orientation.
HSE Prep
Distance learning opportunities are open year-round.
FVCC Kalispell prep classes begin every six weeks and run for five-week cohorts. Classes are Monday through Thursday, 9am-12:30pm. Study hall services are available Mondays after class.
LCC Libby prep classes are available Tuesdays and Thursdays in the morning and evening.
ELL classes are open enrollment starting in July through the end of March. Pre-assessments determine class placement, and online options are also available.
If you are looking for a copy of your High School Equivalency transcript or credential, go to DiplomaSender.com and create an account. You can also contact DiplomaSender directly by starting a chat at DiplomaSender.com or calling 1-855-313-5799.
Find more information here.
Connect with us at

or 406.756.3884

title: English Language Learning
full_text:
English Language Learning
English Language Learning (ELL) classes teach adult, non-native speakers of English to communicate through speaking, reading, and writing. The courses also provide cultural education for living and working in the United States.
Through interactive and engaging activities, students build their English language vocabulary to better integrate into local communities. Students are able to engage more effectively in government, education and workplace environments. The course also provides preparation for the U.S. Citizenship test.
Classes are open to enrollment starting in July and ending in March. Students will complete a pre-assessment to determine class placement, and online options are also available.
For class schedules please email
adulted@fvcc.edu
or call 406.756.3884.
Contact Us

title: High School Equivalency
full_text:
High School Equivalency
Congratulations on taking the next step forward in your career and education by completing your High School Equivalency Test!
FVCC offers both HiSET and GED testing for students to earn a high school equivalency diploma from the Montana Office of Public Instruction.
Montana residents 19 years or older may register for tests at
hiset.org
or
ged.com
. Montana residents age 16 to 18 must contact us for paperwork requirements prior to registering for testing.
HSE Preparation
The Adult Education Program offers prep courses year-round to help build the skills needed to be successful on all HiSET and GED subject tests: reading, writing, science, social studies, and math.
The class covers academic skills as well as career exploration and goal setting in order to support students moving from HSE completion into college and the workforce. Classes are offered in Kalispell and Libby, with testing support options available for students in Lake or Sanders counties.
To be eligible to take the HiSET or GED, students must be 16 years of age or older, complete paperwork requirements and have officially withdrawn from high school. Pre-registration for class is required.
How to Start
Email
adulted@fvcc.edu
or call 406.756.3884 for information, paperwork requirements and to register.
Attend orientation and take the Test of Adult Basic Education (TABE).
Start studying!
16yo checklist for FVCC
(PDF)
16yo checklist for LCC
(PDF)
17+ years old checklist
(PDF)
High School Withdrawal Letter
(PDF)
Homeschool Withdrawal Letter Template
(PDF)
The Test of Adult Basic Education is a diagnostic test used to determine an individual’s skill level and aptitudes. We test the areas of math and reading. Many students use the scores from this assessment to determine areas they are ready to test in on HSE tests.
New student orientation covers required paperwork, program options and expectations, HSE testing information and account setup, online homework program signup, services offered, scholarship information and TABE pre-testing. Once we have TABE pre-assessment scores, we will help create an individualized learning plan. Distance learning options are also available.
FVCC Class Schedule
5 week classes: Monday through Thursday, 9am-12:30pm
Study Hall: Monday through Thursday after class
LCC Class Schedule
Morning Classes: Tuesdays and Thursdays, 8:30am-11:30am
Evening Classes: Tuesdays and Thursdays, 5:30pm-8:30pm
We have many students whose lives are busy or students who live in rural areas, and they cannot attend classes on campus during the times offered. We provide both in-person and distance learning opportunities and will work with you to prepare for tests at times which fit your schedule. Contact us to discuss your options.
Students 16 years of age must participate in our classes. Students 17 or 18 must submit proper documentation to our program before being cleared for HSE testing on our campuses. Anyone 17 and up has the option to take the HSE tests on their own, and can also take the TABE pretest to determine if services are needed.
The HiSET tests students’ knowledge in math, reading, science, social studies and writing. Students must obtain a combined score of 45 to pass the HiSET.
The GED tests students’ knowledge in language arts, math, science, and social studies. Students must obtain a score of 145 on each test to pass.
When you are ready to start testing, visit
hiset.org
or
ged.com
to create an account, register and pay for testing. Your name and date of birth on your account must match your government-issued photo ID.
Check your account for testing locations and available dates. All tests are computer-based. In-person testing is available at FVCC and Libby campuses.
On testing day, make sure to bring your valid government-issued photo ID and $5 per test receipt from the Business Office to the testing center.
For any other questions regarding test sign up, please contact us at
adulted@fvcc.edu
.
GED: Computer-based tests are $26 each for 4 tests totaling $104. GED fees are paid through your GED account when testing is scheduled.
HiSET: Computer-based tests are $15 each for 5 tests totaling $75. HiSET fees are paid through your HiSET account when testing is scheduled.
An additional $5 per test is due to the Business Office on testing day for both HiSET and GED.

title: Workforce Training & Professional Workshops
full_text:
Workforce Training & Professional Workshops
The FVCC Continuing Education Center and Workforce Training Program provides high-quality, relevant training opportunities that address the needs of local employers. Through local economic, community and workforce partnerships, training programs are consistently adapted to meet business and professional needs.
Through the Continuing Education Center and Workforce Training Program, professionals have access to high-quality and affordable professional development and personal growth courses.
Businesses may also coordinate organization-specific training workshops addressing custom development needs by working closely with the Continuing Education Center staff.

There are many credit and non-credit programs at FVCC that can provide the specific related training instruction (RTI) for an apprenticeship for your business or organization. Learn more about
apprenticeship opportunities

title: Customized Team Training
full_text:
Customized Team Training
Teams that learn together work better and more effectively for your organization. Let FVCC help you create the team training that you need to succeed.
FVCC’s customized team training allows your business or organization to achieve your goals and benefit through increased operational efficiency, improved employee skills and morale, and a shared common mission.
Consider how they can support the goals you have for your business or organization!
If you would like to create training that will meet your specific, unique training objectives, FVCC Continuing Education offers Customized Training options.
Here are some success stories:
A local non-profit wanted to elevate the level of customer service in the organization. We customized an existing 3-hour program for their front-line staff and created a new 3-hour program for their supervisory staff.
A local bank wanted to create a career development program for future potential leaders in their business. We paired a variety of industry trainers and faculty with administrative team leaders from the bank to create a 9-month series.
A local timber company needed their operators to achieve a higher level in their licensing and knew other businesses in the area would also benefit from such training. We created the curriculum they needed, had it approved by the State, and led every student to pass their licensing exam.
We’d love to match your business training objectives with a skilled instructor or industry trainer.
View the FVCC Team Training brochure
.
Please call or email Jodi Smith, Workforce Training Director 406.756.3833 or email
jsmith@fvcc.edu
.

full_text:
Meeting Facilitation
Invite a skilled facilitator to help you prepare and guide your meeting or retreat agenda to support topics from strategic planning to difficult discussion topics to brainstorming innovative change.
A facilitator objectively oversees the meeting schedule, neutrally raises important questions and allows participation by all team or committee members.
Meeting facilitation includes three hours of meeting time to develop an agenda, prepare for the meeting and write a meeting summary.
3 hours | $500
Additional time will be invoiced at $100/hour facilitation time; $50/hr preparation time
Please call or email Jodi Smith, Workforce Training Director 406.756.3833 or email
jsmith@fvcc.edu
.

Jodi Smith earned her Facilitation Skills Certificate through the Association for Talent Development (ATD).

title: Conference & Retreat Planning
full_text:
Conference & Retreat Planning
Considering hosting a conference or large event?
The FVCC Continuing Education Center can assist with conference registration. You can expect quality assistance to coordinate registration and related conference and retreat planning services for you.
Basic registration services include:
Registering participants in our software system
Processing registration payments including checks, and credit cards
Printing enrollment rosters
Compiling accounting summary of funds.
Intermediate registration services include the basic services plus:
Preparing name badges
Maintaining course and instructor information
Awarding Continuing Education Units or other pre-approved certification requirements
Assisting with check-in at conference
Advanced registration services include the basic and intermediate services plus:
Copying handout materials at cost
Inserting handout materials into folders
Additional services such as catering referrals, media equipment, or facility information may be available upon request.
Contact the Continuing Education Center at (406) 756-3832 or email at
ceinfo@fvcc.edu
for fee schedule and additional information.

title: Continuing Education
full_text:
Continuing Education
Discover a passion. Sharpen a skill. Enrich your life. Continue your lifelong journey of learning with FVCC Continuing Education.
Summer 2026 Catalog
View Summer 2026 Catalog
(PDF)
View and Register for Classes
Current Instructors Portal
Courses are offered on a spring, fall and summer timeline with different classes available each season. Space is limited so register early to ensure your spot.
Payment is required at the time of registration.
Adults age 65 or older may receive a $5 discount on tuition fees for non-credit classes that cost $30 or more. Special programs may not qualify for this discount.
Reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities can be arranged by contacting the Continuing Education Center.
Unless otherwise noted, all classes are held on the FVCC campus. Check your receipt and email reminder for class location.
Classes that do not receive sufficient enrollment may be canceled.
If a class is canceled, you will be notified and a full refund will be issued.
Cancelations received 14 calendar days prior to the start of a class or workshop will receive a full refund. Students must contact Continuing Education at 406.756.3832 or
ceinfo@fvcc.edu
to cancel and request the full refund.
No refund is issued for cancellations received less than 14 calendar days prior to the start of the class or workshop.
Continuing Education classes at Flathead Valley Community College provide intriguing and engaging opportunities for life-long learners. We offer a variety of personal development, business and workforce training, and general interest classes and activities.
We publish three catalogs each year. The Continuing Education Curriculum Committee reviews new course proposals on an on-going basis. We welcome applications from potential instructors and professionals with a wide range of educational and life experiences who demonstrate subject matter expertise and teaching capability.

Fall Semester (Sept-Dec):  May 15

title: Kids College
full_text:
Kids College
Spend your summer discovering new worlds at Flathead Valley Community College with fun, hands-on activities and extraordinary teachers at FVCC Kids College.
FVCC’s Kids College is a summer program for students ages 6-18. Kids College consists of week-long classes designed to stimulate creative minds, build healthy bodies and challenge adventurous souls. With subjects in art, science and technology, language and culture, and computer game design, there is something for every young learner!
Summer 2026 Registration
View Summer 2026 Catalog
(PDF)
View and Register for Classes
Kids College will run the weeks of:
June 15-19
June 22-26
July 6-10
July 13-17
July 20-24
Important Reminders
Parents or a designated adult must sign the child in/out each class day.
Please arrive no earlier than 5 minutes prior to the start of class.
Plan to pick up your child/children immediately at the end of class. If you will be delayed, notify the Continuing Education Center at 406.756.3832. Your child will be brought to the office for you to pick up.
Kids College is not an all-day camp. Your child/children are NOT supervised on campus during the lunch hour.
Students are expected to behave in a non-disruptive manner during and after class and may be dismissed from a class for behavior violations.
Only registered students are allowed in class.
Students are responsible for their own belongings. If items are left behind, they will be taken to the Continuing Education Center office.
Students may bring water and a snack and should wear appropriate clothing including closed-toe shoes or sandals.
Cell phones must be turned off during class.
Find information on
cancellation and refund policy
and other Continuing Education policies and procedures.

title: Senior Institute
full_text:
Senior Institute
The Senior Institute at FVCC is for community members 65+. The Senior Institute is a vibrant community of older adults who are excited to learn, build relationships, and explore new ideas. From long-time returning students to those new to FVCC, the Senior Institute is a welcoming community for anyone who is interested in learning new skills and growing their social network. Join us in the upcoming Senior Institute to see what the excitement is about!
Senior Citizen Discount for FVCC Credit Courses
Adults 65 years of age and up who are residents of Flathead and Lincoln counties receive discounted tuition and fees when registering for credit courses. See the current
Tuition and Fees Schedule
for more information.
Spring 2026 Edition
Fall 2026 Edition
Fridays, February 13-March 20, 2026
Classes 1-3:05pm
Program and refreshments 3:30-4pm
In-person registration and registration processing begins January 28 at 8am.
See the
Spring 2026 class schedule
(PDF). Enrollment is limited.
Check back here this summer for upcoming Fall 2026 dates.
Registration Steps
Submit the registration form and payment selection to:
FVCC, 777 Grandview Drive, Kalispell MT 59901
Email to: registrationinfo@fvcc.edu
Drop off the completed form to the Student Center
Complete a
non-degree application for admission

full_text:
Campus Dining
You can't go wrong with lunch in the Eagle's Nest or a study session over coffee at Common Grounds. Try both fresh, local choices next time you're on campus.
The Eagle's Nest
Looking for a bite to eat on campus? Drop by the Eagle’s Nest, serving fresh meals from local produce harvested on the Campus Farm when seasonally available.

Open Mon-Fri, 8am-2:30pm

For a coffee or tea and a cozy space to study, visit the Common Grounds Cafe located in the Broussard Library for warm and cold beverages, snacks, and fresh-baked pastries.

Open Mon-Fri, 8am-2:30pm

full_text:
Student Engagement
Student Engagement at FVCC provides opportunities for students to get involved with the local community, campus clubs, and leadership organizations. You'll find your niche at FVCC and connect with other students. Get the most out of your college experience and join us today!
Student Government & Leadership

title: Student Government & Leadership
full_text:
Student Government & Leadership
Do you enjoy helping others, have fresh, creative ideas and want to gain leadership skills while making a positive impact on your fellow students’ college experience? The following opportunities might be for you.
Student Government
Student Government advocates in the interest of all students, provides leadership opportunities and enhances the college experience by unifying and empowering its members.
The group is fiscally responsible for student activity fees and welcomes any questions or concerns regarding allocation of appropriate resources.
To get involved or get more information, connect with
studentgovernment@fvcc.edu
.
Student Leadership Programs
FVCC’s Student Leadership Program supports student leaders by building leadership skills, awareness of leadership styles and theories, and encourages personal growth through a variety of experiences and practice.
All students are welcome to participate at no cost. If you currently hold a leadership position or are interested in becoming a leader of a campus organization, this program is designed for you.
The kickoff to our Student Leadership Program begins with a two-day retreat in the fall with transportation, lodging, food and materials provided at no cost to current students.
Leadership workshops are provided throughout the year to further develop leadership skills and challenge you to apply the skills you've learned to real-world situations. The program ends with a celebratory awards ceremony in May.
Connect with the Student Engagement Office at
student-engagement@fvcc.edu
or 406.756.3908 for more information.

title: Student Organizations
full_text:
Student Organizations
FVCC offers a wide variety of student organizations that promote and enhance student success. We encourage you to explore and enjoy exciting new experiences, build lifelong friendships and make every moment of your college experience memorable.
Art Club
The Art Club is committed to furthering education and inspiration for developing artists and the community.
Advisor
: David Regan | 406.756.3993 |
dregan@fvcc.edu
Chess & Games Club
The Chess Club provides a safe, inclusive and fun environment and welcomes all students regardless of skill level.
Advisor
: Russ Lamson |
rlamson@fvcc.edu
Disc Golf Club
The Disc Golf Club is dedicated to growing the sport on campus by creating a fun, inclusive, and supportive community for players of all skill levels while organizing both recreational and competitive events that promote good sportsmanship.
Advisor
: Ranell Mueller |
rmueller@fvcc.edu
Firearms Enthusiasts Guild
Firearms Enthusiasts Guild is open to all students interested in improving their knowledge of firearms safe handling, technical expertise and historical significance. The group also engages in activities to promote the Firearms Technology program.
Advisor
: Michael Garcia |
mgarcia@fvcc.edu
First Gen Club
Our mission is to foster connections and a sense of belonging for first-generation students.
Advisor
: Jennifer McKinney |
jmckinney@fvcc.edu
Genders & Sexualities Alliance
The GSA is an inclusive group that provides a social support, serves the community and spreads awareness.
Advisor
: Beth Romain | 406.756.3923 |
eromain@fvcc.edu
Honors Club
Open to all FVCC students who are interested in or currently enrolled in the Honors Program.
Advisor
: Marty Mullins |
mmullins@fvcc.edu
Intervarsity Christian Fellowship
A campus ministry that provides students the opportunity to be part of a small group bible study, outreach events, strong loving community and missional activities. The club invites students into caring, diverse communities where they can build friendships and explore the Christian faith.
Advisor
: Janice Heil | 406.756.3373 |
jheil@fvcc.edu
Mask and Hammer (Theatre Club)
The theatre club helps provide support to and promote all theatrical events and theatre students at FVCC. The club seeks to engage students in all aspects of theatre including acting, set design, lights, sound and production.

rhaptonstall@fvcc.edu
Music Club
The music club is committed to bringing the joy of music to the students of FVCC.
Advisor
: Nicole Sanford | 406.756.4813 |
nsanford@fvcc.edu
Native American Student Association
The Native American Student Association supports the academic and personal success of American Indian/Alaskan Native students and all FVCC students with an emphasis on promoting cultural awareness and creating a welcoming campus.
Advisor
: Leslie Greene | 406.756.4358 |
lgreene@fvcc.edu
Phi Theta Kappa
Phi Theta Kappa
(PTK) is a national scholastic honorary society for two-year colleges. The purpose of Phi Theta Kappa is to recognize and encourage scholarship among college students.
Advisor
: Molly Maxwell |
mmaxwell@fvcc.edu
Student Government
Student Government
advocates for student needs, organizes entertainment and social opportunities and provides financial and practical support for campus student organizations and clubs.

wjeschke@fvcc.edu
Table Top Role Playing Games Club
Whether you're a seasoned adventurer or brand new to games like Dungeons & Dragons, TTRPG Club is the perfect place to explore.

kkissane@fvcc.edu
Turning Point USA (TPUSA)
TPUSA aims to educate students on fiscal responsibility, free markets and constitutional rights through campus activism, academic resources and open, thought-provoking discussion.

Veterans Association
This is a supportive social organization developing a foundation of understanding between veterans and non-veterans. All students, veterans and non-veterans are encouraged to join.
Advisor

title: Phi Theta Kappa
full_text:
Phi Theta Kappa
Phi Theta Kappa (PTK) is an international honor society for two-year colleges whose mission is to recognize and encourage scholarship among college students. Since 1918, this prestigious organization has provided professional and leadership development, scholarship access and engagement opportunities for more than 3 million members worldwide.
Membership
Students who achieve outstanding academic achievement are eligible for membership. Criteria include the completion of 12 semester credits and a minimum grade point average of 3.4.
FVCC’s local chapter, Alpha Iota Pi, provides members with opportunities for individual growth and development through participation in the four hallmarks of Phi Theta Kappa: scholarship, leadership, fellowship and service.
Benefits
Scholarship
: Because membership demonstrates a commitment to scholarship, PTK provides more than $37 million in scholarships to four-year colleges and universities to support transfer students.
Leadership
: PTK members have many opportunities to develop leadership skills, such as serving in an officer position or chairing a committee.
Fellowship
: Members are provided several opportunities to participate in social activities throughout the school year.
Service
: Phi Theta Kappa is a dedicated service organization. Members participate in activities designed to improve their colleges and their local communities.
Commencement
: Each Phi Theta Kappan is entitled to wear a distinguished gold and navy blue stole and tassel at commencement. These items can be purchased or earned through active participation in your chapter.
For more information, contact:
Wendy Jeschke
Program Director, Student Engagement
Molly Maxwell
Associate Professor, Math

Student Lounge
The Student Lounge is located in the Student Center (SC) 102 and provides a comfortable, open space for you to gather, study and relax between classes. It's open M-F 8am-5pm.
The lounge also hosts a variety of activities, like ping pong and video gaming tournaments, a pool table, bingo and trivia. It’s a great place to meet your friends and make new ones!

title: Campus Recreation
full_text:
Campus Recreation
Campus Recreation offers a wide variety of events designed to help you enjoy your time on campus and build lifelong connections with your peers.
Events include skiing, hiking, climbing, ice skating, intramural sports, bowling, painting events, bingo, trivia, video game tournaments, movie nights and more!

title: Service Learning & Volunteer Programs
full_text:
Service Learning & Volunteer Programs
Service learning is an educational opportunity for students, faculty, and community partners to work together to enhance student learning while meeting the needs of the community. Through meaningful service activities and structured reflection, students gain valuable hands-on experience, leadership skills, and a sense of lifelong civic responsibility.
Students serve 15 hours at a nonprofit organization, school, or government entity directly related to the learning outcomes of their course. Upon completion, students receive a service learning indicator on their official transcript and appropriate credit for their class.
Service Learning & Volunteer Opportunities
Some courses incorporate service learning as part of their curriculum. Besides influencing your grade, it also gives you hands-on experience, opportunities to explore career fields, and upon successful completion of hours a service learning indicator on your official transcript.
Student Agreement and Liability Form
Complete and submit the
Volunteer Agreement and Liability Form
to the student engagement office (Blake Hall 155) or email at
student-engagement@fvcc.edu
.
MobileServe Account
Once you submit the form above, you will receive an email to complete an account for MobileServe. You will need your student ID number to finish creating the account. MobileServe will be used to track and verify your volunteer hours.
Find a Community Partner
Each course that participates in service learning has specific learning outcomes that relate to the class. You are required to serve for 15 hours at a nonprofit, school or government entity that directly relates to learning outcomes found in the course syllabus. To find a community partner, log on to your MobileServe account, click on “Events” on the left dashboard, click on “Discover” and search locally for opportunities. Click on an opportunity you are interested in, select “I Want to Help” and reach out to the organization contact person to set up a time to serve. Having trouble finding a community partner? Ask your professor! They will most likely have a few in mind.
Track Your Hours
Log in to MobileServe and select “Log Your Hours.” An email will be sent to the organization supervisor to verify them.
Service Learning Indicator
Once the 15 hours are complete, submitted, and verified on MobileServe your professor will be able to view them and a Service Learning Indicator will be placed on your official transcript. This will show universities that you have made a difference in your community and have valuable hands-on experience that will help you be successful in your college career and beyond.
Service learning is a way to enhance your courses by using a hands-on teaching methodology, and it gives students the opportunity to fulfill unmet needs within the community. To participate in the service learning program, follow the steps below:
Faculty Interest Form
Request a faculty interest form by emailing service-learning@fvcc.edu and submit it to FVCC’s student engagement office (Blake Hall 155).
MobileServe Account
Once you submit the form above, you will receive an email to complete an account for MobileServe. With this account, you will be able to track your student’s hours and verify appropriate organizations. Organizations must be a nonprofit organization, school, or government entity that ties directly with the learning outcomes of your course.
Student Reflection
Incorporating a way for students to reflect on their service is a vital portion of service learning. This can be an essay, journal, discussion, presentation, etc.
Student Hours
MobileServe will be used to track student service learning hours. Once the student logs their hours on their MobileServe account, an email will be sent to the community partner to verify the hours.
Service learning is a great way to fulfill the unmet needs within your organization while giving students the opportunity to enhance their learning with hands-on experience. If you would like to be a Community Partner with FVCC’s Service Learning program, follow the steps below:
Community Partner Questionnaire
Submit a completed
Community Partner Questionnaire form
to wjeschke@fvcc.edu or turn it into the student engagement office (Blake Hall 155).
VolunteerMatch Account
Create a free account on
volunteermatch.org
Post Organization Needs
Post opportunities for students to sign up for. Without posting students will not be able to see your organization. For information on how to post opportunities for students, visit
vmhelp.zendesk.com
Connecting With Students
Students will see the opportunities on
MobileServe
, an online platform that allows verification and tracking of hours. When students sign up for opportunities on MobileServe you will be able to discuss times with them and fulfill your organization needs.
Tracking Hours
MobileServe will be used to track student volunteer hours. Once the student logs their hours on their MobileServe account, an email will be sent to you to verify their hours.

title: Community-Based Work-Study
full_text:
Community-Based Work-Study
The Community-Based Work Study program is designed to give students an opportunity to earn their financial aid awards (work-study funds) through employment at off-campus community service agencies such as local non-profit organizations, government entities and schools. The program provides students with the opportunity to make meaningful contributions to local organizations, learn about community needs and develop professional skills.
This program operates during the academic year only.
The Community-Based Work Study program allows you to earn work study funds through an off-campus non-profit organization. Students may earn $12–$14 per hour and can work between 8-19 hours per week depending upon award.
Eligibility
In order to be part of the program, you must qualify for state or federal work study funding and be able to work a minimum of 8 hours per week.
How to Apply
Check your work study eligibility. Students eligible for work study funding should receive an award letter from the FVCC Financial Aid Office prior to the start of the semester. You can also check your work study eligibility by contacting the financial aid office in LRC 117 or by calling 406-756-3850.
Finalize your class and availability schedule, accounting for any extra-curricular activities you may have.
Review and apply for student jobs at the
FVCC Human Resources Student Employment page
.
Next Steps
Someone from the student engagement office will contact you to schedule an interview.
After you have received and accepted an offer, you will be notified by the human resources office to complete the on-boarding process.
Important Things to Know
The amount of work study award that you have been awarded is the amount that you are eligible to earn during the school year. You cannot earn more money than your award. Any money not used by the end of the school year is lost and does not carry over into the next year. The program does not continue into the summer.
There are a limited number of spaces available to participate in this program. You need to work hard to secure a job in a timely fashion if you hope to participate.
You must work a minimum of 8 hours per week to participate in the CBWS program.
You may work up to 19 hours per week during the times that FVCC is in session. You do not have to work over semester or spring breaks, but you may choose to do so and are eligible to work 40 hours per week during these times if you are pre-registered for the following semester. Work with your site supervisor to determine your schedule during the breaks. You cannot continue your work into the summer months.
You are ultimately responsible for keeping count of the amount of money that you earn and ensuring that you do not work in excess of the amount of work study money you’ve been awarded. Determine in advance how you will spread your hours over the course of the year to align with your work study award. Figure your hours properly; your organization will depend upon you once you’ve started working for them.
You must commit to an organization for the full year.
If you have issues, you need to try to work them out with your supervisor at your site. You can contact the Student Engagement Coordinator for support, if needed. Should you choose to leave a job, you are expected to give at least two-week notice.
Please note that it is a privilege and not a right to participate in the CBWS program. Agency supervisors ultimately determine if they think you’d be a good match for the organization or not and also have the right to dismiss you for any reason (barring discrimination). The Student Engagement Coordinator also determines whether you’re an appropriate fit for work in the community in FVCCs’ name.
As this is a federal program, there is a very strict division between church and state. Regardless of the organization you are working for, you may not practice religious proselytizing, care for church property, or become involved in partisan politics. Your agencies are aware of this, and should not ask you to participate in such activities.
Your site supervisor will be asked to complete an evaluation of your work before the close of each semester.
We will also ask that you provide us with formal feedback about your experience working at your organization at the close of the spring semester.
Please feel free to come see Wendy Jeschke at any time regarding your Community Based Work Study experience. Wendy can be reached at (406) 756-3908 or at
wjeschke@fvcc.edu
or in LRC 102.
Flathead Valley Community College’s Community-Based Work Study program can assist your organization fulfill its mission.
How It Works
Students are available to work between 8-19 hours per week at your agency.
Eligibility
In order to be eligible with no financial responsibility, your organization must:
Be a federal, state or local public agency or a private nonprofit organization.
Employ students as reading tutors for preschool-aged children or elementary school children; or
Employ students as math tutors for children in elementary school through ninth grade; or
Employ students to perform family literacy activities in a family literacy project that provides services to families with preschool or elementary age children; or employ students in community service activities, and is performing civic education and participation activities in community service projects.
Identify a direct supervisor and provide supervision during scheduled work hours.
In order to be eligible with a 10% cost share, your organization must:
Be a federal, state or local public agency or a private nonprofit organization.
Sign a statement that FVCC does not own, operate, or control the organization or agency.
Provide a signed letter stating that the agency cannot afford to pay the regular non federal share (30%).
Identify a direct supervisor and provide supervision during scheduled work hours.
How To Apply
In order to be considered as a host organization in the Community-Based Work Study program, please complete the
Community Partner Questionnaire
and send it to Wendy Jeschke at wjeschke@fvcc.edu or (406) 756-3908.
After receiving your form, someone from the student engagement office will contact you.
Community Mentor Program
The community mentor program focuses on connecting FVCC students with members of the community that work in the student’s career field of interest. Students will be given a chance to learn what it is like working in their field of interest and to know what is required of them to enter that field. They will also be able to develop their professional network and become more engaged in the community.
The goal of the meetings is to help you grow and keep track of your professional progress in your field of interest. You should meet a minimum of four times throughout the course of the year in a work setting or public space. To participate follow the steps below:
Complete the
Community Mentor Student Application form
and submit it to the student engagement office (LRC 102) or scan and email to
student-engagement@fvcc.edu
.
Based on your submitted form we will match you with a mentor.
Meet for the first time and complete the
Community Program Goals
form together and sign the Community Mentor Program Expectations form.
Meet at least three more times throughout the year.
After the last meeting, complete the Student Evaluation form.
The goal of the meetings is to help your mentee progress in their field of interest. You should meet a minimum of four times throughout the course of the year in a work setting or public space. To participate follow the steps below:
Complete the
Mentor Application form
and submit to the student engagement office in the Learning Resource Center (LRC) room 102, or scan and email to
student-engagement@fvcc.edu
.
Based on your submitted form we will match you with a mentee.
Meet for the first time and complete the
Community Mentor Program Goals form
together and sign the
Expectations and Accountability form
.
Meet at least three more times throughout the year.
Complete the
Community Mentor Check-in form
after the second meeting.
After the last meeting, complete the Mentor Program Evaluation form.
If you have any questions please email
student-engagement@fvcc.edu
, call (406) 756-3908, or stop by the student engagement office at Flathead Valley Community College, Learning Resource Center (LRC) room 102.

title: Voter Registration
full_text:
Voter Registration
FVCC encourages students to participate in all local, statewide and federal elections to increase representation of student voices in government and foster lifelong habits of civic engagement.
Register, find info on candidates and issues, navigate voting laws, and turn out at the polls.
Mail in voter registration forms are located in the Student Center 102. More information and an online form can be found here:
Flathead County
Lincoln County
Not sure where you are registered to vote? Find out here.
Get Involved
Upcoming Events on Campus
September 17: Voter Registration Day & Constitution Day
October 7-11: Voter Education Week
October 29: Vote Early Day
November 5: Election Day
More Resources
Become civically engaged and volunteer to be an election judge
Learn more at Montana Election & Voter Services
To get involved or for more info, contact Wendy Jeschke at
wjeschke@fvcc.edu
or 406.756.3908

title: Student Housing
full_text:
Student Housing
Built in 2017, FVCC’s student housing in Founders Hall offers comfortable single and double bedroom units with full kitchens and bathrooms at competitive rental rates on campus. All units are equipped with secure entrances.
The living space also features coin-operated laundry facilities, private study and meeting areas, mailboxes, community spaces, bike racks and free parking. Safe walking paths allow residents easy access to the rest of campus, as well as to nearby stores and restaurants.
Apply for Housing
Room assignments are offered in order of application completion. All completed applications will be placed on a waitlist until guarantees/room assignments are made.
Studio Apartment (single)
Two Bedroom (shared)
